6: vehemin baYHWH wayyachsheveha lo tsedaqah (wĕheʾĕmīn bY̌HWH wayyaḥšĕbehā lô ṣĕdāqāh): 'And he believed YHWH, and he counted it to him as righteousness.' The most theologically dense sentence in Genesis. Heamin (believed/trusted): Hiphil of aman — not merely intellectual assent but the full posture of reliable trust, the same root as amen. Wayachsheveha (counted/reckoned/credited): the accounting verb used in financial and legal contexts — credit assigned. Tsedaqah (righteousness): the right-relational standing before God that Abraham receives not by doing but by trusting. Paul (Rom 4:3; Gal 3:6) and James (2:23) both cite this verse as definitional for faith-righteousness; their different emphases (Paul: before circumcision, therefore not by works; James: completed by the Aqedah of ch. 22, therefore enacted by obedience) are both grounded in the canonical shape of Genesis.
1:
אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה (ʾaḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh) — "after these things." ʾAḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh is a recurring narrative connective in Genesis (22:1, 20; 39:7; 40:1; 48:1) that signals a significant event following a major one — the Melchizedek episode and the Sodom-king's offer are the preceding context; the covenant ceremony follows.

אַל-תִּירָא אַבְרָם אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ — "do not be afraid, Abram; I am your shield." The divine "do not fear" (ʾal-tîrāʾ) formula is the standard angelic and prophetic reassurance (cf. 26:24; Isa 41:10, 13, 14; 43:1, 5; Dan 10:12, 19). Māgēn (shield) — God as military protector; the same root as māgan in 14:20 ("delivered your enemies"). The shield image is explicitly military: having just refused Sodom's goods and made enemies of the Elamite coalition, Abram may fear reprisal. שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד — "your reward/wages very great." Śāḵār (reward, wages, compensation) — God himself is the reward; the doubled intensifier harbēh mĕʾōḏ is emphatic.
2: אֲדֹנָי יֱהֹוִה מַה-תִּתֶּן-לִי — "Sovereign LORD, what will you give me?" The double divine address ʾAḏōnāy YHWH (Lord YHWH) is the form of heightened supplication used in lament and petition (Deut 3:24; Josh 7:7; Judg 6:22; Ezek 2:4; 4:14; Amos 7:2, 5). Abram complains to God — a bold but covenant-legitimate speech act. וְאָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ עֲרִירִי — "and I am going childless." ʿArîrî (childless, stripped) — from ʿārar (to strip bare, to lay waste); the LXX reads ametatithetos; the word implies desolate barrenness, not merely the absence of children. The heir by default is Eliezer of Damascus — a household servant who would inherit by ANE custom if the master died childless (attested in Nuzi adoption contracts).
3: בֶּן-בֵּיתִי יוֹרֵשׁ אֹתִי — "a son of my house will be my heir." Ben-bêtî (son of my house) = household servant; yôrēš (heir, one who inherits) from yāraš (to possess, inherit, dispossess). Abram states the problem plainly: without a biological heir, his estate and the covenant promise will pass through a servant. The word of YHWH must address this directly — which it does in v. 4.
4: לֹא יִירָשְׁךָ זֶה כִּי-אִם אֲשֶׁר יֵצֵא מִמֵּעֶיךָ — "this one will not be your heir, but one who comes from your own body." ʾĂšer yēṣēʾ mimmēʿêḵā (who comes forth from your inward parts/loins) — meʿeh (inward parts, bowels, the seat of emotion and reproduction) is the most intimate bodily term possible for biological descent. The promise eliminates the servant-heir category entirely: the covenant passes only through biological offspring.
5: הַשָּׁמַיְמָה וּסְפֹר הַכּוֹכָבִים — "look toward the heavens and count the stars." The locative haššāmaymāh (toward-the-heavens, with directional heh) and the imperative sĕpōr (count!) stage a visual-rhetorical challenge: "if indeed you can count them." The night-sky is then announced as the metaphor for the offspring (kōkāḇîm, stars) — the most sublime natural image of innumerability. The star metaphor here complements the dust metaphor of 13:16 and the sand metaphor of 22:17. The LXX follows the MT closely; Paul cites this verse in its LXX form in Rom 4:18.
7: אֲנִי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִיךָ מֵאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים — "I am the LORD who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans." The divine self-identification formula ʾănî YHWH (I am the LORD) is the same as the Sinai declaration of Exod 20:2 ("I am the LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt") — the Ur/Exodus parallelism is structural: YHWH defined himself to Moses by the Exodus just as he defines himself to Abram by Ur. The verb hôṣēʾtî (Hiphil perfect: I brought out, I caused to go out) marks the completed past act of divine election — the calling is already accomplished.
8: בַּמָּה אֵדַע כִּי אִירָשֶׁנָּה — "how shall I know that I will inherit it?" Abram asks for a sign — immediately after faith has been credited to him (v. 6). This is not a contradiction but a feature of OT faith: asking for confirmation does not negate trust, it specifies its object. Compare Gideon's signs (Judg 6:36-40), Hezekiah's sign (2 Kgs 20:8), and Mary's question to Gabriel (Luke 1:34) — all are signs requested within the context of genuine faith. God answers not with rebuke but with a covenant ceremony.
9: קְחָה לִי עֶגְלָה מְשֻׁלֶּשֶׁת — "take for me a heifer three years old." Mĕšulleššet (three-year-old, tripled) — the animals specified (heifer, goat, ram each three years old, plus a turtledove and young pigeon) represent a combination of large and small animals used in various sacrificial contexts. Three-year-old animals are mature, prime animals. The dove and pigeon pair appears in Leviticus purity offerings (Lev 1:14; 5:7-11) — the entire list suggests a complete sacrifice, not a specific rite. The cutting of the animals (v. 10) is the covenant ceremony.
10: וַיְבַתֵּר אֹתָם בַּתָּוֶךְ (waybatter ʾōtām battāweḵ) — "he cut them in two down the middle." Bāttar (to cut in pieces) — a rare verb; the noun bĕtārîm (pieces cut in two) in v. 17 is related. This cutting-in-two is the act that enables the covenant ceremony: the parties pass between the halves, invoking upon themselves the fate of the cut animals if they violate the covenant (cf. Jer 34:18-20: "I will make the men who violated my covenant like the calf they cut in two and then walked between its pieces"). The birds are not cut — a deliberate distinction that has been explained variously (they are too small; the dove/pigeon pair serves a different sacrificial function).
11: וַיֵּרֶד הָעַיִט עַל-הַפְּגָרִים וַיַּשֵּׁב אֹתָם אַבְרָם — "birds of prey came down on the carcasses, and Abram drove them away." ʿAyiṭ (birds of prey, raptors) — the vulture/raptor image in the OT often denotes menace and desolation (Job 28:7; Isa 46:11 where the "ravenous bird from the east" = Cyrus; Matt 24:28 / Luke 17:37). Abram actively defends the covenant-sacrifice from defilement — he maintains the integrity of the ceremony even in the gap before God arrives. Some read the birds as a prophetic symbol of the foreign nations that will oppress Israel during the 400-year sojourn.
12: תַּרְדֵּמָה נָפְלָה עַל-אַבְרָם (tardēmāh nāplāh ʿal-ʾaḇrām) — "a deep sleep fell upon Abram." Tardēmāh (deep sleep, torpor) — the same word as Adam's deep sleep during the creation of the woman (2:21). The word appears in contexts of divine initiative: God acts while the human is unconscious; the sleep signals that the covenant's establishment will be entirely divine. וְאֵימָה חֲשֵׁכָה גְדֹלָה נֹפֶלֶת עָלָיו — "a great dark terror was falling on him." ʾÊmāh (terror, dread) + ḥăšēḵāh (darkness) + gĕḏōlāh (great) — the triple intensifiers describe the numinous dread of theophanic nearness (cf. Exod 20:18-21; the disciples' fear at the transfiguration, Matt 17:6; Ezekiel's face-down prostration, Ezek 1:28).
13: יָדֹעַ תֵּדַע כִּי-גֵר יִהְיֶה זַרְעֲךָ — "know with certainty that your offspring will be sojourners." The infinitive absolute yādōaʿ tēḏaʿ (know truly, surely know) — emphatic double of the same root, conveying absolute certainty. What follows is direct prophecy: 400 years of sojourning in a foreign land, slavery, and oppression. Gēr (sojourner, resident alien) — the status Abram himself inhabits in Canaan (17:8; 23:4; Ps 105:12). The offspring will be sojourners in someone else's land — Egypt — as Abram is a sojourner in Canaan. The parallel is intentional.
14: וְגַם אֶת-הַגּוֹי אֲשֶׁר יַעֲבֹדוּ דָּן אָנֹכִי — "but also the nation whom they serve I will judge." Dān ʾānōḵî (I will judge) — the same verb root as the name Dan (Judg 5:10). God promises to judge the enslaving nation — the Exodus will be an act of divine justice against Egypt, not merely rescue of Israel. וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן יֵצְאוּ בִּרְכֻשׁ גָּדוֹל — "afterward they will come out with great possessions." The Exodus plunder of Egypt (Exod 12:35-36) is prophesied here, 400 years before it happens — the vocabulary (reḵûš, goods) echoes the same word used for Abram's wealth in 13:2 and the Sodom spoils in 14:11-12.
15: וְאַתָּה תָּבוֹא אֶל-אֲבֹתֶיךָ בְּשָׁלוֹם — "but you, you will go to your fathers in peace." The personal promise to Abram contrasts with the collective fate of his offspring: they will suffer for 400 years; he will die in peace. ʾEl-ʾăḇōteḵā (to your fathers) is an expression for dying and being gathered to the ancestors — the clan-death formula that implies connection beyond death. תִּקָּבֵר בְּשֵׂיבָה טוֹבָה — "you will be buried at a good old age." Śêḇāh ṭôḇāh (good old age, a ripe old age) — the covenant life fulfillment formula; Abram dies at 175 (25:7-8) "at a ripe old age, an old man and satisfied with years" — the precise fulfillment of this promise.
16: וְדוֹר רְבִיעִי יָשׁוּבוּ הֵנָּה — "and in the fourth generation they will return here." The "400 years" of v. 13 and the "fourth generation" here have generated considerable discussion: a generation of 100 years would make four generations = 400 years (and the Exodus genealogy for Moses in Exod 6:16-20 is compressed to four generations from Levi). כִּי לֹא-שָׁלֵם עֲוֹן הָאֱמֹרִי עַד-הֵנָּה — "for the sin of the Amorites is not yet full." Šālēm (complete, full) — moral completion as the trigger for judgment. God delays Israel's possession of Canaan by 400 years because the Canaanites' sin has not yet "ripened." The divine patience extends even to the wicked: judgment awaits the full measure of guilt. This principle also implies a moral dimension to the conquest: Israel displaces Canaan not arbitrarily but as a judgment on specific, fully-developed wickedness.
17: תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן וְלַפִּיד אֵשׁ אֲשֶׁר עָבַר בֵּין הַגְּזָרִים הָאֵלֶּה — "a smoking furnace and a blazing torch that passed between these pieces." The theophanic fire symbols — tannûr ʿāšān (smoking furnace/brazier) and lappîḏ ʾēš (torch of fire) — represent the divine presence. The divine fire is also the wilderness pillar (Exod 13:21-22), the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:18; Deut 4:11-12), and the Shekinah (1 Kgs 8:10-11). Critically, only the fire-symbols pass between the pieces — Abram is asleep (v. 12). The covenant is entirely divine in initiation, execution, and guarantee: YHWH alone walks between the animal halves, placing the covenant obligation entirely on himself. This unilateral structure is the grammar of grace — the Abrahamic covenant's unconditional character rests on God's self-commitment alone.
18: כָּרַת יְהוָה אֶת-אַבְרָם בְּרִית (kārat YHWH ʾet-ʾaḇrām bĕrît) — "the LORD cut a covenant with Abram." Kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant) — the idiom derives from the cutting of the animals in the ceremony (vv. 9-10): to cut a covenant is to perform the sacrifice that ratifies it. The land boundaries follow: from naḥal miṣrāyim (the brook/wadi of Egypt, likely the Wadi el-Arish on the Sinai border) to the Euphrates — the full extent of the Davidic-Solomonic empire (1 Kgs 4:21; 8:65). The covenant land exceeds what Israel ever controlled in history, pointing the promise toward an eschatological fulfillment.
19: The קֵינִי (Kenites), קְנִזִּי (Kenizzites), and קַדְמֹנִי (Kadmonites) are listed first in the ten-nation land-promise list. The Kenites appear later as allies of Israel (Judg 1:16; 4:11, 17; 5:24; 1 Sam 15:6). The Kenizzites are associated with Caleb's clan (Num 32:12; Josh 14:6, 14) — Caleb "the Kenizzite" receives Hebron as his inheritance, the very city of Abram's altar (13:18). The list of ten peoples is broader than the standard seven-nation list of the Conquest period (Deut 7:1; Josh 3:10), suggesting an original land promise of greater scope than what the Conquest actually reached.
20: The חִתִּי (Hittites), פְּרִזִּי (Perizzites), and רְפָאִים (Rephaim) continue the land-promise list. The Hittites appear in Abram's own lifetime as the sellers of the Machpelah cave (ch. 23 — Ephron the Hittite); the Perizzites are among the Canaanite peoples whose territory will be Israelite territory (Exod 3:8, 17). The Rephaim appear already as a conquered people in ch. 14 (v. 5) — the covenant land includes territory that even the eastern coalition had swept through.
21: The final group in the land-promise list: אֱמֹרִי (Amorites, the highland Canaanites whose sin is "not yet full" in v. 16), כְּנַעֲנִי (Canaanites), גִּרְגָּשִׁי (Girgashites), and יְבוּסִי (Jebusites — the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem). The ten-nation list frames the promised land as the entire territory of the pre-existing peoples — the Abrahamic covenant is simultaneously a land-grant and a judgment oracle. The moral framework of v. 16 (the Amorites' sin not yet full) prevents the land-promise from being mere territorial ambition: the transfer of the land is grounded in divine moral justice, not ethnic favoritism.
